data.title: Wall Street Lunch: Obesity Pill Progress
data.description:
Listen below or on the go on Apple Podcasts and Spotify

Lilly’s diabetes pill meets Phase 3 trial goal. (0:15) Trump says Fed chief Powell can’t be ‘terminated’ soon enough. (2:27) Take an electric air taxi from Manhattan to JFK. (4:48)

This is an abridged transcript of the podcast:

Our top story so far, Eli Lilly (NYSE:LLY) is rallying after the company said its oral GLP-1 receptor agonist orforglipron reached the main goal in a Phase 3 trial for adults with type 2 diabetes.

Citing topline data from its ACHIEVE-1 clinical trial for a key secondary endpoint, Lilly said that the once-daily pill reduced weight by ~8% on average (16 lbs) at the highest dose in the 40-week trial.

LLY’s main rival in the weight loss drug market, Novo Nordisk (NVO) and other obesity drug developers such as Viking Therapeutics (VKTX) and Amgen (AMGN) are seeing pressure on shares.

Lilly CEO David Ricks said: "As a convenient once-daily pill, orforglipron may provide a new option and, if approved, could be readily manufactured and launched at scale for use by people around the world."

The global trial involved 559 patients with type 2 diabetes who had inadequate glycemic control from diet and exercise alone.

In ACHIEVE-1’s main goal, orforglipron lowered a key diabetes biomarker called A1C by 1.3%–1.6% from a baseline of 8.0% across 3mg, 12 mg, and 36 mg doses in the per-protocol study population, while those on placebo witnessed 0.1% of A1C reduction.

Going the other way is Dow component UnitedHealth (UNH), which is sinking after it slashed its full-year outlook following lower-than-expected Q1 results.

The company revised its 2025 adjusted EPS outlook to $26.00 to $26.50, down from the $29.50 to $30.00 it forecast in December and below the $29.74 consensus.

CEO Andrew Witty said: “UnitedHealth Group grew to serve more people more comprehensively but did not perform up to our expectations. We are aggressively addressing those challenges to position us well for the years ahead and return to our long-term earnings growth rate target of 13 to 16%.”

Looking to the economy, the European Central Bank cut rates by 25 basis points to 2.25%. That’s the seventh reduction since it began easing monetary policy in June 2024.

The ECB statement said: "The euro area economy has been building up some resilience against global shocks, but the outlook for growth has deteriorated owing to rising trade tensions."

The move was expected, and before it was official drew a strong statement from President Donald Trump, who simply can’t believe Fed chief Jay Powell isn’t following the lead of ECB chief Christine Lagarde.

In a post, Trump slammed Powell for not cutting rates – not for the first time – and for Powell’s hawkish speech Thursday where he said the level of tariffs makes the Fed’s dual mandate more tricky. He said Powell’s “termination can’t come soon enough.”

“The ECB is expected to cut interest rates for the 7th time, and yet, ‘Too Late’ Jerome Powell of the Fed, who is always TOO LATE AND WRONG, yesterday issued a report which was another, and typical, complete ‘mess!’,” the president said.

Looking closer to home, the April Philly Fed manufacturing index added to the stagflation conundrum. It plunged to -26.4 from 12.5 in March. That was much lower than the consensus of 6.7.

The 39-point drop was one of the biggest in history, exceeded only by COVID era and Great Recession declines.

New orders fell sharply, from 8.7 in March to -34.2. That’s its lowest reading since April 2020, which at -70.9 was the lowest on record, and just above the second-lowest reading recorded in March 2009 of -39.50.

The prices paid index edged up from 48.3 to 51.0, its highest reading since July 2022 when inflation was around a 40-year-high.

Among active stocks, Evercore maintained its Outperform rating and $250 price target on Apple (AAPL) while highlighting the iPhone maker's supply chain and diversity amid the trade war.

Analyst Amit Daryanani said: "If tariffs are applied broadly, we think Apple would continue to import and push through relevant price increases to offset the cost inflation along with getting suppliers to share the burden."

And Hertz (HTZ) continues to soar after notching a 50% gain in the previous session (we told you about that on the last Wall Street Lunch.)

Pershing Square disclosed that it acquired 4.1% of the car rental company in Q1. With the reported 19.8% stake, Bill Ackman's firm is the second-largest shareholder of Hertz behind Knighthead Capital, which owns 58.9% of outstanding shares.

Bank of America analyst John Babcock highlighted that Pershing Square has launched activist campaigns in the past and could also be looking for an activist role at the car rental company. He thinks the activist investor could seek to push the board for changes in how Hertz operates and request board representation.

In other news of note, here’s a story for anyone who’s been stuck on the Van Wyck.

Archer Aviation (ACHR) announced plans to launch an electric air taxi network in New York City. The eVTOL company's goal is to dramatically reduce travel times between Manhattan and major area airports.

Using its piloted Midnight aircraft, which accommodates four passengers and a pilot, Archer Aviation intends to offer flights from key Manhattan heliports to John F. Kennedy International, LaGuardia, and Newark Liberty International, as well as other regional airports.

The flights are expected to take between five and fifteen minutes, a significant improvement over the one-to-two-hour car journeys often required due to heavy city traffic.

The company's Midnight aircraft, featuring 12 engines and propellers for redundancy, is designed for safety levels comparable to commercial airliners, while also being quieter and producing fewer emissions than traditional helicopters. Notably, Archer is manufacturing the aircraft in the United States and is working with the FAA to secure Type Certification, having already received its FAA Part 135 Air Carrier and Operator Certificate in June 2024.

And in the Wall Street Research Corner, GlobalData TS Lombard thinks things are a little too sunny in trader town.

Strategist Daniel von Ahlen says: “The market isn’t accurately pricing the prospective hit to growth from Trump’s policies.”

He said consensus GDP forecasts of 1.8% for 2024 remain overly optimistic given looming labor market strains and inflationary pressures.

Markets are underestimating the cumulative drag from Washington’s policy shifts, he said, adding that DOGE austerity measures, aggressive immigration crackdowns and China’s retaliatory tariffs threaten to shrink the labor force, squeeze corporate margins, and erode consumer spending power.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: NVDL: Trump's Chip Restriction Is An Opportunity, NVDA Washed Out (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
2025 has been a total flip from some of last year's winning themes. The Magnificent Seven stocks have stumbled, while defensive niches have rallied. Gold keeps working—perhaps the new "Magnificent" stocks are the gold miners, up 55% in total return through mid-April YTD.

The Mag 7 ETF (MAGS) is down about 20%, dividends included, so far on the year. Pressuring the former glamour stocks is NVIDIA (NVDA), but I am turning optimistic about Jensen Huang's company. I see the stock as washed out, considering that it dipped below $90 earlier this month. Ahead of its earnings report in May, and after troubling news regarding its H20 chip, I am upgrading the GraniteShares 2x Long NVDA Daily ETF (NVLD) from a hold to a buy.

I was neutral on the trading vehicle in early February, but there have been material changes since that analysis. I asserted patience then, and shares are down 19% compared with the S&P 500's 10% drop.

Mag 7 Has Been Out of Favor, That May Change As Equities Bounce

According to the issuer, NVDL seeks daily investment results, before fees and expenses, of 2 times the daily percentage change of the common stock of NVIDIA. The fund should not be expected to provide 2 times the cumulative return of NVDA for periods greater than a day. Leveraged ETFs are generally riskier than traditional ETFs, which do not employ leverage. As GraniteShares states, "The fund is designed to be utilized only by knowledgeable investors who understand the potential consequences of seeking daily leveraged (2X) investment results, and are willing to monitor their portfolios frequently."

For my usual cautionary statement, per the issuer, "For periods longer than a single day, the fund will lose money if the underlying stock's performance is flat, and the fund may lose money even if the underlying stock's performance increases over a period longer than a single day. An investor could lose the full principal value of their investment within a single day."

Here is an illustration of how negative compounding returns occur in a leveraged ETF: Suppose an index starts at 100, and the leveraged product also begins at 100. If the index rises by 10% to 110, the 2x long product increases to 120. However, if a subsequent 10% drop happens, the index falls to 99 (a 1% loss from the initial value). In contrast, the 2x long fund declines to $96 (0.8 x 120), reflecting a 4% decrease. For a deeper understanding of the risks tied to leveraged ETFs, you can refer to authoritative bodies such as the SEC. More information can be found in the updated Investor Bulletin titled: Updated Investor Bulletin: Leveraged and Inverse ETFs.

NVDL has lost some assets under management in the past two and a half months. AUM now stands at $4.05 billion, down from $4.9 billion in early February. The ETF's share price momentum has soured from an A+ three months ago to a weak F ETF Grade by Seeking Alpha's quantitative scoring system today. Carrying a 1.06% annual expense ratio, NVLD is a trading product, not meant for a strategic, long-term position. It does not pay a dividend.

Liquidity is strong—average daily volume is just shy of 30 million shares, while the 30-day median bid/ask spread is 8 basis points, per GraniteShares. NVDL is, of course, a highly risky ETF, given a 120% annualized volatility figure.

I suppose my bullish turn on NVDA and NVDL is a bit contrarian. Notice in the chart below that NVDA is in a clear downtrend, with a series of lower highs and lower lows since the $151 all-time high at the turn of the year. The YTD low under $90 came on big volume and dismal sentiment, and it looks like a tradable low to me, so upgrading the 2x long ETF is appropriate.

NVDA Hit a Washout Low in April Under $90

Consider that NVDA dropped 40% from its high tick to the low 1-minute print this year. While we've seen greater drawdowns in the past, NVDA's size today and highly profitable business should limit further losses.

NVDA Historical Drawdowns

I feel confident in that assertion due to NVDA's valuation. To be clear, NVDL is a short-term trading vehicle, so whatever NVDA's P/E ratio is, it should not weigh heavily into the investment decision. Nevertheless, now with a forward earnings multiple below 20, I believe value-buyers could step in and more than offset the lack of demand from momentum players.

S&P 500 P/E Heat Map: NVDA Under 20x Earnings

On an earnings-growth-adjusted basis, NVDA is very cheap. Out-year EPS could verify above $5.50, which makes NVDA shares attractive for a range of investor types. The bearish H20 chip restriction news earlier this week notwithstanding, Huang has made strides to appease President Trump, so I would not be surprised to see NVDA catch benefits from the White House in the weeks ahead, particularly in light of Trump's pivot on his once-stern tariff stance.

The backdrop is no doubt concerning: Along with a possible $5.5 hit from the export curbs, ASML (ASML) reported weak order numbers this week, China's GDP growth slowed, and there's a real chance corporate capex, including semiconductor demand from the hyperscalers, could weigh. So it's by no means an "all clear."

NVDA: Revenue & Earnings Forecasts, EPS Revision Trends

Big picture, after an awful start to the year for the Mag 7, a rotation back into tech would make sense, assuming the April low under 5000 on the S&P 500 was a longer-term low. Historical rebounds have generally favored tech over other sectors. Moreover, the Mag 7 trade with a collective P/E under 23x—the cheapest valuation in more than two years. This is a broad intermarket tailwind potential.

Mag 7 P/E at a Two-Plus Year Low

The above J.P. Morgan Asset Management graphic jives with the below look from Goldman Sachs. Their team argues that the Mag 7 has a slight P/E premium to the global stock market, but it's deserved given overall sales growth.

Mag 7 P/E Justified Given Sales Growth - GS

It's difficult to imagine that Mag 7 EPS growth will get dinged too badly when zooming out to see the long-term earnings growth trend. Perhaps the biggest risk, and what makes it different this time, is that China's Deepseek AI announcement in Q1 could have changed the landscape for the AI trade writ large. It's a risk to monitor.

Mag 7 EPS Momentum Will be Hard to Disrupt

For NVDA specifically, its fiscal Q1 2026 ends this Sunday, and the first quarter earnings report is confirmed to take place on Wednesday, May 28 AMC. The company's management team is slated to present at a slew of industry conferences, workshops, and summits before the reporting date. So, I expect volatility to continue to run high, which is an opportunity for NVDL traders.

NVDA: Corporate Event Risk Calendar

Zooming in on the earnings report next month, the options market prices in a 7.9% earnings-related stock price swing when analyzing the at-the-money straddle, expiring soonest after the reporting date. Shares moved smack-dab on the options market last time, falling 8.5% after another bottom line beat.

NVDA: An 8% Earnings Move Expected, A String of EPS Beats

Finally, NVDA has historically performed very well in May after lackluster Aprils. So, calendar trends augur well for a bounce in the stock. You can potentially turbocharge a bullish stance with the 2x long NVDL product. Long with a stop under the $86 April low appears as a favorable risk/reward play today with the stock price under $105 as of this writing. A rally through $105 on NVDA would be particularly bullish.

NVDA: Very Bullish Seasonality On Tap

NVDA: Technical View

The Bottom Line

I am upgrading NVDL to a buy. I see the stock as having hit a capitulation low earlier this month. The recent bearish news regarding China's restriction on the H20 chip is a chance to get long. NVDL offers 2x daily long exposure, which would further benefit from bullish trends through May.

data.title: I Wouldn't Touch Axon Stock With A 10-Foot Pole (Technical Analysis)
data.description:
Intro

Axon Enterprise, Inc. (NASDAQ:AXON) stock has held up relatively well during the market selloff, with six month gains still totalling around 33%. In the below analysis, many technicals signal a bit of uncertainty and indecision but overall, an upside breakout does seem more likely from here. For the fundamentals, Q4 results were robust overall, but their full year 2025 guidance may be of some concern. Revenue and adjusted EBITDA growth is expected to slow considerably, signalling a tougher year ahead for the company. The biggest red flag for me is the valuation. While revenue growth is expected to slow to multiyear lows, the P/S ratio has remained far above average. In addition, the massive premium in excess of 1500% versus the Industrials sector is inexplicable with the fundamentals not nearly enough to support this kind of valuation. Therefore, while technicals do favor some upside from here, I personally would not touch this stock with a ten-foot pole.

Technical Analysis

The Basics

Trends, Support & Resistance

Axon has not been in much of a trend in the past few months. Since late February, the stock seems to have been in a bit of a trading range. This is a positive, given that many stocks are in a downtrend due to the tariff induced global selloff. Currently, there are three major support levels underneath the stock, compared to only one resistance level. The nearest support is at the 550 level, as the stock bounced there in early January as part of a bullish reversal. The next support area is at 500, a key round number. This level held as support in late February and early March. There is also support in the high 460s. An upside gap that occurred in November was at this level, and that day was the highest volume by far in that month. This level was support earlier this month as well. The sole resistance level is at around 593 as a downside gap in late February occurred there. The volume on that day was also the highest in that month and as you can see, the stock has already found resistance there on two occasions.

Moving Averages & Indicators

The 50-day SMA has been above the 200-day SMA for the past six months as a long term bullish signal, but the gap between them has been on the decline since January. Therefore, upward momentum seems to be stalling for Axon. However, the 200-day SMA seems to have contributed as support earlier this month as a key sign of strength in the stock. In addition, the stock has broken back above the 50-day SMA as another bullish sign. For the near term, the 9 day EMA just had a bullish crossover with the 21 day EMA, and so the outlook for the near future looks strong for the stock.

The Bollinger Bands midline has not really acted as support nor resistance in the past month and a half or so. This supports the conclusion that the stock is bound in a trading range. The stock is currently above the midline, and so that perhaps suggests that odds favor an upside breakout as of now. For the MACD, there was a bullish crossover just recently. The gap between the indicator and its signal line has also expanded, as shown by the bullish histogram. For the RSI, the indicator bounced back above 50 earlier this month to show net bullish control. The current reading is 56.44 which shows that the bulls are in control by a decent amount. Lastly, for the stochastics, there was a bullish crossover just earlier as the %K surged above the %D. Both lines went on to break above 50 and then 80 to show that the bulls have a tight grasp on the stock at this point.

Takeaway

Overall, I would say the technicals indicate a resilient stock amid the market selloff. The chart shows the stock being in a trading range but with more support than resistance, odds likely favor an upside breakout. The moving averages also show some promising signs. While the gap between the SMAs is still shrinking, the stock has fought back above the 50-day SMA as a clear signal of long-term strength. The EMAs also had a bullish crossover just now. In addition, all of the indicators show bullish signals and so, in my view, risk is to the upside currently according to these technicals.

Fibonacci

Retracement

Using the February peak and the April low as the inputs, potential Fibonacci Retracement targets are shown in the above chart. As you can see, the stock has fought above the 23.6% and 38.2% levels and seems to have found some resistance at the 50% level. Note that this is basically the same area as the February downside gap's resistance, and so it is understandable why resistance in the 590s is so strong. If the stock is able to break out from this area, the next target would be the 61.8% level, which seems to be at around the 620s price range. Overall, the stock's ability to break past the first two retracement levels is a sign of strength, in my view.

Ichimoku Cloud (Advanced)

Axon has broken into the cloud on the past two trading days, as the stock is now above the Leading Span A. This shows that bearishness may be behind the stock at this point. However, the Leading Span B is still likely to be strong resistance, especially as it sits right at the key 600 round number. The cloud itself is bearish into May and the thickness of the cloud is considerable moving forward. With cloud's downward trajectory, if the stock is unable to stage an upside breakout from here, it may see bearish pressure into late May. For the near term, the Conversion Line and Base Line are now at the same level, which could signal some indecision. The Conversion Line was below the Base Line recently, however, and so momentum has become neutral after being bearish. Lastly, the Lagging Span has broken above the historic stock price of early March, and so upside momentum is likely present in the stock currently. Overall, I believe there are some promising signs in the Ichimoku Cloud, but there are still signs of uncertainty that warrant caution. The stock hasn't broken out above the cloud, the bearish cloud is thickening into late May, and the Conversion and Base Lines are at the same level, showing some indecision in near term momentum.

Fundamental Analysis

Earnings

Quarterly Results

Axon reported their 2024 Q4 earnings in late February and showed resilient results as a whole. Revenues were reported to be $575 million, up 34% YoY, while adjusted EPS was $2.08, up 84.1% from $1.13 in the year ago period. Their TASER segment led the way with revenues growing 37.1% YoY, while their Software and Sensors segment reported a still highly respectable 31.6% growth rate. Operating cash flow was also strong, as the $250 million figure is a 79% YoY increase. Both revenue and EPS also surpassed consensus expectations, as they beat by $9.11 million and $0.68, respectively. As you can see in the above chart, revenue growth is below average due to the spike in growth in their 2022 Q4. Other than that quarter, their 2024 Q4 growth rate is at around average and in-line with the past couple of quarters. For operating cash flows, this quarter represents the highest level in the figure in the past few years, and so the business is clearly still trending in the right direction. Lastly, adjusted EBITDA increased by a healthy 55.6%, showing an impressive increase in profitability in the business.

Guidance

Axon only provided full year 2025 guidance and did not provide an outlook specific to their Q1. For 2025, they are expecting to see revenues of between $2.55 billion and $2.65 billion, and so the midpoint represents growth of 25%. Their full-year revenue for 2024 was $2.08 billion and was a 33.3% increase over 2023's revenues. Therefore, this guidance shows a growth slowdown is expected for the company. They also provided guidance for adjusted EBITDA, as they are projecting between $640 million and $670 million for the figure. 2024 adjusted EBITDA was reported to be $521.4 million and so at the midpoint, this guidance implies growth of 25.6%. 2023 adjusted EBITDA was $331.4 million, and so 2024's growth rate for this figure was 57.3%. Therefore, this guidance also shows an expected slowdown for Axon's growth as 2025 continues to play out. Overall, the outlook provided here is softer than desired, and so a tougher year may be ahead for Axon.

Valuation

Price To Sales Ratio [P/S]

The P/S ratio has shown some contraction from late 2024 highs but remains at far above average levels. It currently has a reading of 21.80 after starting the year at about 24. I believe this multiple shows overvaluation in Axon stock. As I discussed earlier, revenue growth is currently below average. Furthermore, guidance shows that full-year revenue growth will slow to only 25%. Therefore, growth in the upcoming quarters is expected to dip to far below average levels compared to the past few years. While this is the case, the P/S ratio has remained at far above average levels, signalling a disconnect with the fundamentals of the business. In my view, the recent modest contraction in the ratio has done little to ease the overvaluation in the stock. In addition, the Industrials sector currently only has a median P/S ratio of 1.33 and so Axon is at a 1539% premium to peers according to this measure. With annual growth expected to moderate significantly, this valuation premium is literally a head scratcher for me. While their leadership position in security solutions may help them to command an above average valuation, this high of a premium is inexplicable in my view.

Conclusion

Before looking at the valuation, Axon seemed to be a net buy in my view. The technicals were still a bit mixed, but overall, an upside breakout from the trading range seems more likely. Most recent earnings were also highly respectable, with most financial metrics showing fundamental strength. While guidance was a bit weaker than desired, 2025 is still likely to be an overall resilient year for the business. The valuation, however, is a major deterrent for me. The P/S ratio is detached from reality with its far above average reading. Furthermore, as stated above, the premium versus the Industrials sector seems highly excessive. As a result, while the technicals do seem overall attractive currently, I initiate Axon at a sell rating on the basis of gross overvaluation in the stock.

data.title: Scholar Rock: Apitegromab Approval And Obesity Shot Make For Rating Upgrade
data.description:
Investment Overview

I last covered Scholar Rock Holding Corporation (NASDAQ:SRRK), the Cambridge, Massachusetts-based biotech, in a note for Seeking Alpha back in December last year. I gave its stock a "Hold" rating, concluding:

Scholar Rock is currently valued as if its lead asset will be approved, and achieve at least $500m revenues per annum by 2030, in my view.

If you are a strong believer in the "myostatin can help preserve lean muscle mass in combo with GLP-1 drugs" thesis, then buying Scholar Rock at today's share price may well appeal to you as a calculated gamble.

For anybody with a more moderate conviction, however, the best option here is to play a waiting game. If early in 2025, Scholar Rock stock has drifted to, let's say <$30 per share (its price does tend to dip quickly after rapid gains), the reward may be considered worth the risk of buying stock, and risking an approval setback or a Phase 2 obesity failure.

At the time of publishing that note, Scholar stock traded at $44 per share, and today, as I suggested it might, it trades at $30 per share. Hence, this feels an apposite time to be providing an update.

Apitegromab Progress - Approval A Near Certainty?

First of all, Scholar's lead candidate is apitegromab, which the company discusses as follows in its 2024 annual report / 10K submission:

an investigational, fully human monoclonal antibody that inhibits myostatin activation by selectively binding the pro- and latent forms of myostatin in skeletal muscle and is being developed for the treatment of spinal muscular atrophy (“SMA”).

In my last note, I mapped apitegromab's journey from its stunning Phase 2 data readout in 2020, which triggered a surge in the share price from ~$15, to $65 back in early 2021, through data disappointments and safety setbacks, that dropped it back <$10 per share, to a Phase 3 readout in October that sent stock soaring back >$40.

To update, Scholar has now submitted a Biologics Licence Application ("BLA") for apitegromab, which has been accepted by the Food and Drug Agency, and given a priority review, shortening the review period from ~10 months, to ~6 months. The Prescription Drug User Fee Act ("PDUFA") date - the date by which the FDA must decide whether to authorize or reject the drug for commercial use, is September 22nd, 2025. Scholar has also submitted a Marketing Authorization Application ("MAA") to the European Medicines Agency ("EMA").

Scholar says in a press release:

Apitegromab’s regulatory submissions are based on positive efficacy and safety data from the pivotal Phase 3 SAPPHIRE trial (NCT05156320), for which the Company reported positive topline data in October 2024, as well as supportive data from the Phase 2 TOPAZ trial and long-term extension ONYX trial. The results from SAPPHIRE, including primary and secondary endpoint analyses, were featured in multiple clinical presentations at the 2025 Muscular Dystrophy Association (MDA) Clinical & Scientific Conference earlier this month.

There are three approved therapies for SMA, a devastating muscle wasting disease. Roche's (OTCQX:RHHBY) Evrysdi (risdiplam), approved in 2020, and earning $1.8bn of revenues last year, Biogen's (BIIB) Spinraza (nusinersen), approved in 2016, and earning $1.6bn last year, and Novartis' Zolgensma (onasemnogene abeparvovec-xioi), approved in 2019 and posting revenues of ~$1.2bn last year.

Apitegromab is a different kind of therapy, designed to be used in combination with a gene therapy drug, and being a "negative modulator of muscle growth, as opposed to a gene therapy like Evrysdia, Spinraza and Zolgensma, that (according to a Scholar presentation):

specifically and only inhibits myostatin, and has the potential to build muscle and strength to improve patient outcomes.

As such, Scholar believes that apitegromab, administered by intravenous infusion, may have peak revenue potential of >$2.5bn. Management believes its ability to improve motor function over time is superior to its gene therapy rivals, and provides supporting data - outperformance over nusinersen - as per the slide below, which shows nusinersen versus apitegromab plus standard of care (likely nusinersen):

In terms of the market opportunity, Scholar's Chief Commercial Officer ("CCO") told analysts on the company's Q4 earnings call with analysts:

97% of patients surveyed by Cure SMA identified improving muscle strength as an important need that they want to see from a new SMA treatment. From our own market research, we know that more than 80% of treating neurologists agree that preserving muscle should start as early as possible in SMA...74% of neurologists recognize that in the future, a combination of modalities to target the motor neuron and the muscle will be necessary to treat SMA.

Furthermore, management sees apitegromab's label being expanded into indications such as Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis ("ALS"), Duchenne Muscular Dystrophy ("DMD") and others, creating a multi-billion dollar franchise in time.

Some analysts have suggested the drug could achieve ~$1.2bn in peak revenues in SMA, but we should note that gene therapies are highly expensive - Spinraza apparently costs >$800k for the first year of therapy, and $380k per year thereafter - therefore Scholar will have to a find a price that ensures it can accommodated into a treatment regime, which could make achieving "blockbuster" sales (>$1bn per annum) challenging. Other analysis suggests apitegromab revenues may peak at <$500m, more than half-way through the next decade.

We may also note that both Roche and Biohaven (BHVN) have late stage studies of myostatin inhibitors ongoing - Roche is hoping to achieve approval after 2027, however, and Biohaven's candidate, taldefgrobep, appears to be following a similar time-line.

The GLP-1 / Myostatin Inhibitor Opportunity - Crystallised, Or Cloudy?

Now let's consider the progress of the EMBRAZE study, which is attempting to position apitegromab as a potential obesity therapy, in combo with a Glucagon-like peptide 1 receptor agonist ("GLP-1 RA").

Readers may be aware of Novo Nordisk's (NVO) Wegovy (semaglutide) and Eli Lilly's (LLY) Zepbound (tirzepatide) and their striking ability to reduce weight in patients by ~15% - 25% in 50-72 weeks, and analysts' expectation that these drugs and other GLP-1 RA's will control a >$120bn - $200bn market by 2030.

Scholar's contention is that it is paramount that patients are losing the right kind of weight, and that weight is not put back on as soon as medication is topped, and that apitegromab, or its next-generation candidate, SRK-439, can assist with this.

IN Q2, Scholar has promised a "proof-of-concept" data readout for apitegromab plus tirzepatide, and in Q3, an Investigational New Drug ("IND") application for SRK-439 (IND approval is required before in-human clinical studies can begin). The below slide explains the data we are likely to see sometime this quarter:

The success of tirzepatide has added some ~$500bn to the market cap of Lilly, which is now the world's most valuable Pharma, valued at ~$700bn at the time of writing. Imagine, therefore, what a successful Phase 2 data readout would do for the share price of Scholar - a $2.8bn market cap company at the time of writing.

Furthermore, should the apitegromab data disappoint, Scholar can turn to SRK439, a drug that it says has "exquisite selectivity for myostatin, and which preclinical data has suggested has the potential to achieve "dose-dependent lean mass preservation."

Analysis - My Updated Take On Scholar Rock Stock

With shares now trading at the value at which I said I'd be tempted to invest in my last note, am I upgrading my former "Hold" call to a "Buy"?

It's a tricky call, in my view. Although FDA - and perhaps EMA - approval for apitegromab in SMA now looks highly likely in the case of the former, and likely in the case of the latter, a commercial launch will be costly, and although Scholar reported a cash position of $437m as of the end of 2024, it also reported a net loss for the year of $(246m).

Commercial revenues from apitegromab in 2025 will be minimal and therefore I can see Scholar needing to raise funds in early 2026, which will be dilutive to existing investors and drag the share price down.

Regarding the obesity opportunity, it's hard to say if the apitegromab / tirzepatide readout will be positive - management appears to be more focused on SRK-439, which may suggest it lacks confidence in the original combo. Succeed, and a share price surge feels inevitable, but fail, and it will be a long wait for SRK-439 data of any kind, and in this fiercely competitive market, Scholar may be left behind.

These are the negatives I'd consider carefully before making any investment into Scholar - we may also note a worrying trend of biotech's share price failing to respond positively to commercial approvals, as the market frets about ability to deliver in commercial markets against established "Big Pharma" incumbents, Roche, Biogen and Novartis' in Scholar's case.

Nevertheless, I am going to have the courage of my convictions and upgrade my rating to a "Buy" because ultimately, I believe the positives outweigh the negatives. I suspect that apitegromab is a potential blockbuster, potentially in SMA alone as it performs a function no other drug can in terms of building muscle.

That in itself is enough to support a ~$3bn valuation, in my view, even if a fundraising is completed, so in that regard, we could consider the GLP-1 opportunities as "free hits," with massive upside potential and limited downside potential. In this post I have not even mentioned a third candidate, Linavonkibart, which Scholar calls:

an investigational inhibitor of the activation of latent transforming growth factor beta-1 (“TGFβ1”), that is being developed for the treatment of cancers that are resistant to anti-PD-(L)1 antibody therapies

I have not discussed the fibrosis or hematology candidates either, although until they are successful in the clinic, we should not attach too much value - only potential.

Personally, I believe the biotech bear market has weighed heavily on Scholar's share price so far in 2025, and at the very least, the company could recapture lost gains and trade at the ~$45 per share mark before the end of 2025, thanks to the market opportunity in front of the lead candidate.

As such, there is a >50% upside opportunity that feels achievable, and substantially more if the Phase 2 obesity data is positive. Like any biotech investment, there are clear risks that must be considered, but also a significant upside opportunity in play, and I marginally favor the reward over the risk now that shares have fallen to my target price of $30.

data.title: Sinopharm Group: Mixed Read-Throughs From Peer Disclosures And Management Guidance
data.description:
I continue to have a Neutral opinion of Sinopharm Group Co. Ltd. (OTCPK:SHTDY) (OTCPK:SHTDF) (1099.HK) or "SGCL".

Its liquidity- and margin-related ratios were analyzed with the previous November 21, 2024 update.

My Hold rating for SHTDF is retained. This considers the unfavorable commentary from other Chinese healthcare distribution companies and SGCL's guide indicative of relative outperformance.

Peers Have A Negative View Of The Sector

Shanghai Pharmaceuticals (OTCPK:SHPMF) (OTCPK:SHPMY) [2607:HK] and China Resources Pharmaceutical (CRPHF) [3320:HK] or "CRP" are distributors of medical products in the PRC ("People's Republic Of China"). They are the main listed comparables for SHTDY.

Their disclosures suggest that the industry is in difficult times.

In 4Q24, SHPMY issued an announcement summarizing its conversations with the buy-side and sell-side. It mentioned the local pharm industry is "under pressure" and "experienced negative growth" last year.

SHPMF subsequently noted at the recent fiscal year earnings call on April 2 that its profitability was hurt by lower drug prices pertaining to Volume Based Procurement ("VBP"). Its FY24 distribution business GPM ("Gross Profit Margin") contracted by -58 bps ("basis points") to 5.9%.

China's latest 10th VBP exercise, whose results will take effect from 2Q25, had over a hundred medicines being priced at less than one-tenth of what they sold for previously.

On the other hand, CRPHF revealed in its investor presentation published in late-March '25 that the company is taking a longer time to collect cash. Its DIO ("Days Inventories Outstanding") metric for the distributor segment was lengthened by six days in 2024. This meant that it needed as long as 3 months to turn sales into cashflow.

At the earlier Morgan Stanley (MS) "China BEST Conference", CRP highlighted that the PRC's "anti-corruption campaign" is now is "more routine" with sales representatives encountering "more restrictions in hospital access." I obtained these management comments from MS's January 9, 2025 research note titled "Conference Feedback" which is only accessible for clients.

I think it's understandable that CRPHF's unsold products are piling up. Chinese hospitals have become more cautious in procuring new medical devices with increased regulatory scrutiny.

SHTDF is dealing with similar problems as its peers, looking at the numbers. Its cash conversion cycle increased by 7 days in FY24. The company suffered from a -0.6 ppts ("percentage points") decline in its gross profit-to-revenue ratio for the prior year.

It's fair to say that the sector is a troubled one, and Sinopharm won't be unaffected.

But SHTDY Could Gain Share Judging By Its Outlook

SGCL welcomed a new president "Mr. Wanyong Lian" at the end of August-2024.

Wanyong knows the company and the pharma-related operations very well. He was with SHTDF between 2009 and 2017. Prior to assuming this new role, Mr Lian was the head of another domestic distributor Shyndec.

Sinopharm reports its financials on an semi-annual basis. Its leader formally communicated with analysts for the first time at the FY2024 results briefing ("transcript not publicly available") on March 24.

Management guided at that event its 2025 topline expansion will be superior to that of the broader sector. There could be a change in focus with the leadership change. In my view, SHTDY has the intention ("implied by guidance") and ability to grab a bigger slice of the domestic medical distribution pie.

Sinopharm, SHPMF, and CRPHF boasted market shares of 23%, 10%, and 8%, respectively. These numbers are sourced from MS' January 5, 2025 report "China Healthcare 2025 Outlook".

The three major players should benefit from sector consolidation, while SHTDF is also likely to win more business than its closest rivals.

This is a fragmented industry with 40 percent of revenue derived from sub-scale entities. These companies' respective revenues are under 1% of the entire market.

Also, SGCL, China Resources, and Shanghai Pharma are all State-Owned Enterprises ("SOEs"). They might have an edge in closing deals with other government healthcare institutions. It could be also easier for them to apply for loans from SOE banks in the country.

My take is that a growing proportion of the industry's sales will be concentrated in the hands of the "Big Three".

Separately, it's highly probable that SHTDY will do better than the company's key competitors for two reasons.

One is that Sinopharm is a pure-play distributor, so its leadership can devote all their attention to the core business. Both CRP and SHPMY have drug development divisions.

The other factor is that SGCL has a scale advantage. All else equal, you would probably have greater confidence in the largest player selling your medical products.

Notwithstanding sector-wide issues detailed in the preceding section, Sinopharm has the potential to achieve financial improvement. This will be realized with share gains and the resulting positive operating leverage effects.

I'm projecting a +4% topline increase and a +10 bps higher net margin for Sinopharm in FY25. This would be a decent set of results in the challenging environment. The company's revenue decreased by 2 percent in 2024, and its net profit-to-sales metric also narrowed by -30 bps during the same period.

The Final Word

I still have a Hold for SHTDY, taking into account industry issues and company-specific merits.

The name is also fairly valued based on a PEG ("Price--To-Earnings Growth") of 1x ("6.6/6.5").

As per S&P Capital IQ data, Sinopharm trades at 6.6 times forward P/E, while its consensus FY24-27 bottomline CAGR estimate is +6.5%.

A peer valuation comparison exercise for SHTDF is inappropriate. I noted above that the other two listed companies have non-distribution operations.

data.title: Micron: Trade War Could Lead To A Stock Collapse
data.description:
Currently, Micron Technology, Inc. (NASDAQ:MU) trades below our latest price target of $74.10 per share. That price target was revealed along with the valuation model in our latest article on the company which was published in January. Following the 27% decline of Micron’s stock since January, we believe that the downside could be even greater as the tariff wars made us change some assumptions in the model, which now yields an even lower price target. That is why Micron remains a SELL for us today.

The Trade War Changes Everything

The recent escalation of the global trade war, triggered by new tariff measures imposed by the Trump administration poses a growing threat to Micron’s operation stability. The incoming implementation of tariffs on chips could disrupt the company’s supply chain and affect its performance this year and beyond. Given the company’s reliance on international manufacturing and sales, particularly in Asia, trade policy risks could lead to the contraction of margins in the not-so-distant future.

We also believe that in addition to the potential supply chain issues, Micron’s business could also face indirect effects from tariffs such as frozen procurement cycles, inventory buildup, and risk-off capital rotation. The analysts on Wall Street share a similar view as they outline Micron as one of the companies that are most at risk from tariffs.

Also, Micron’s DRAM and NAND revenues in Q2 were already down 4% Q/Q and 17% Q/Q, respectively. Considering that segments such as consumer, mobile, and automotive memory will likely remain under pressure as a result of the new tariff policy, we believe that it’s a matter of time before orders for chips will decline sharply due to rising costs. This could become a major issue for Micron, which already has a relatively weak gross margin of 34%. Since other semiconductor names like Broadcom Inc. (AVGO) and NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA) have much better gross margins, it makes sense to believe that Micros is indeed one of the companies that are most at risk from tariffs.

Also, the company still expects to pour around $14 billion into capital expenditures. We view a record CapEx at a time of one of the greatest macroeconomic uncertainties in the last 5 years as a major issue. If the demand for chips falls while costs rise as a result of tariffs, Micron’s ROIC on its record investments might not justify the record spending in the end.

While the company already lost a third of its value in the last six months, we might enter a period of a prolonged downturn as a result of the trade war and the worsening of relations between Beijing and Washington. This could create an additional downside for Micron’s shares and push them to lower prices.

Risk To Our Bearish Thesis

There are still some positive factors that need to be considered. If somehow the trade war deescalates and Washington improves its relations with Beijing, then the aggressive growth of the semiconductor industry, which was predicted in recent months, might resume. This could be a positive thing for Micron since it could lead to the rise in demand for DRAM and NAND memory chips, which are essential to powering large-scale AI workloads. It could also help the company avoid the potential contraction of margins that might happen in the foreseeable future.

The Real Value of Micron

After analyzing the impact that the trade war might have on Micron’s performance, we proceeded to construct a new valuation model to estimate the company’s true worth in the current environment. Our latest model from January which was published before the trade war showed Micron’s intrinsic value to be $74.10 per share. With the number of challenges that the trade war is going to create for Micron, we now believe that the intrinsic value is even lower.

We create the new model when Micron is trading at $69.55 per share. The perpetual growth rate in the model is 3%, which is a rate that we use most of the time when creating valuation models for other businesses. Our projection covers the next five years, and the data like cash and total debt was taken from the most recent earnings report. The discount rate in the model is 7.44%. We calculated it by figuring out Micron’s cost of equity and cost of debt. For the cost of debt calculation, we mostly used its TTM numbers. For the cost of equity calculation, we used a risk-free rate of 4.40%, a beta of 1.18, and a market return rate of 7.77%.

In our forecast table, we expect a gradual decrease in the sales growth rate due to the negative impact of tariffs. That is why in the table the sales forecast for the following years is also lower in comparison to our latest model from January. However, our sales forecast is not that far away from the expectations. While the TTM EBIT margin is 19.5% thanks to the strong performance in the first half of the fiscal year, we expect a contraction of that margin by the end of FY25 and in the upcoming years as well because of the negative impact of the trade war.

The tax rate is 15%, which is the same rate used in the previous article. The CapEx for FY25 is around $14 billion, which is what the management expects this year and what was mentioned earlier in this article. After that, we don’t expect the company to spend over $14 billion annually because of the trade war that will likely affect the economy and make it impossible to justify higher spending. The change in NWC is 1% of sales, which is the same rate we used in our previous model on Micron.

Given these assumptions, our valuation model estimates that Micron’s enterprise value is $71.3 billion. It also shows an equity value of $64.5 billion, which translates to an intrinsic value of $57.45 per share.

Final Thoughts

The trade war has worsened our outlook for Micron. While the company remains a significant player in the y and storage chips market, its performance is likely going to suffer because of the trade war. Given the rise in CapEx, the forecasted contraction of margins, and the normalization of the sales growth rate in the upcoming years, we don’t view Micron as a decent investment right now. Investors may be better served in higher-quality, less cyclical semiconductor names that have less exposure to trade war risks. This is the reason why we still rate Micron as a SELL.

data.title: VåR Energi: Premium Pricing Is Normalizing, I'm At 'BUY'
data.description:
Dear readers/followers,

Over the past year, I've invested more in oil/energy, specifically European/international oil and energy companies. These companies have included businesses like Eni (E), TotalEnergies (TTE), and others, including some Norwegian local and international businesses. I have positions in both Equinor (EQNR) and Aker BP (OTCQX:AKRBF), a smaller company, but nonetheless interesting that I also follow on Seeking Alpha.

Today, however, I'm going to take a look at one of the arms of Eni S.p.A., the one called Vår Energi (OTCPK:VARRY).

The company has not exactly been a good investment overall over the past few years, owing to a decline of over 30% in that time. However, as the company has been declining in share price has declined, the valuation has obviously grown more attractive.

It's with that in mind that I will be covering the company and its basics here to see what, if any, upside is present in the investment, and why I haven't, as of yet, bought into the company despite a compelling yield and a compelling coverage for that yield.

Vår Energi generates over $2.5B worth of revenue and employs over 1000 people. It's headquartered in attractive Norway, and its latest results, which we'll take a look at here from about 1.5 months ago, are more attractive than the market price would suggest.

Let's look at what we have here.

Vår Energi - Undervalued at almost 9% native yield?

Some basics to begin with that may explain why the company is doing so poorly, comparatively.

The company doesn't hit estimates - it has a 100% failure rate in forecasts, even if those forecast statistics are only 2-3 years of data at this point. The company also has an excessively long-term debt to capital on paper, nearly 99% LT debt/cap, which also colors this company's fundamentals. The company typically pays a very high yield - nearly all of its earnings, and usually more than 100% of its adjusted earnings, go towards paying the dividend (Source: F.A.S.T. Graphs Paywalled Link). However, as I will show you in the article, the company uses different metrics, and there's something to be said for those, given that S&P Global and rating agencies obviously seem to accept them.

That being said, the company is BBB rated (straight BBB) and is backed by some solid finances. It was established in -18 when Eni Norge, a part of the Italian multinational energy company Eni, merged with local Point Resources AS, and the merger created one of the largest independent O&G companies in the entire region, with a production capacity of 300k/mbbl per day.

Furthermore, Vår Energi has not been idle. They bought Exxon Mobil's (XOM) upstream assets, all of them, in the NCF for a total of $4.5B, and this included interests in a significant number of producing fields on the NCF, including attractive Grane, Fram, and Snorre.

This has made Vår Energi AS the second-largest operator after Equinor, even larger than Aker BP.

So, Vår Energi, despite being treated as small, is not small. Its objectives are the exploration and production of oil and natgas on the NCF, the so-called Norwegian Continental Shelf. It has interests in both operating and non-operating fields at this time.

It should come as no surprise to you that the company's primary correlation is the oil and gas price, bar none. The base case for more oil and gas is good.

NCS is attractive. Why is NCS attractive? Because NCS production is cheaper, with lower emissions, and has a lot of resources. Furthermore, it's close to Norway, one of the richest countries per capita in the entire world.

While the current geopolitical climate does a good job of "coloring" this outlook, I have never lost sight of the fact that this is a very attractive source for European energy - and neither has Norway or Europe, looking at the size of export and the support given. That is also why significant capital of mine is invested in companies operating here.

Vår Energi has a solid record of delivering growth - not only in production, up from 169 kboepd in 169, to 280 in 2024 compared to 2018, with reserves up from 900 billion boe to 1.2 billion, but also the important ROACE, up from 16% to 20% in 2024 since the company's founding.

The company uses EBITDAX as a metric, which is somewhat unorthodox - it's essentially earnings before interest, tax, D&A and exploration expenses, somewhat questionable but also logical for a company like this - and the annual EBITDAX has risen from $2.7B in 2018, to almost $6B in 2024 - and the company isn't using $3B a year in exploration expenses (Source: Vår Energi IR)

I therefore say that Vår Energi is creating value, and you can see how here.

I believe that in this increasingly uncertain geopolitical world, it will pay off to own local producers of energy, and this is one of them for me, where I live. It would be the same logic as owning local producers in the US if I lived there.

Furthermore, as EU reliance on Russian gas has lowered, and I view it questionable if Nord Stream is ever being restarted, the company's gas position is getting more and more important as well.

So, the basics for this company are solid, I will argue. The company's 2024 delivered strong results, as most EU-based oil and gas companies did, with increased production, growing reserves, and reduced unit OpEx. That unit OpEx is one of the key ratios you want to look at. It means that Vår Energi could essentially "survive" with prices below $15/bbl net of logistical costs, at a unit OpEx of $12.8/boe, which is better than guidance. The company's official breakeven is at about $35/boe, which is only beaten by some Arabic and African producers, as well as some Russian ones. Not many others in Europe can produce at this cost/rate.

Several large key projects are nearing completion as well, and the company's discovery rates have been good, at 50%, adding almost 85 mmboe to the company. The company has responded by paying 30% of its post-tax CFFO in dividends for the year, and for 2025E it already has very lofty targets, including a dividend raise.

The company is targeting an FCF potential of $5-$9B between 2025-2030, of which 25-30% will be returned to shareholders, while becoming Co-neutral in 2030.

Those are some impressive positives, aren't they?

So, where are the risks?

Near-term or fundamental liquidity isn't necessarily an issue, despite some of the leverage ratios from some perspectives. The company has an average debt maturity of over 5 years and $1.3B in available liquidity, which is enough to fund operations well. To counter the leverage, the company is calculating a leverage ratio of 0.8x at year-end 2024, based on net-interest-bearing debt, including leases on an R12 EBITDAX. The danger here is that this ratio could obviously take hits from unsuccessful exploration and the like - still, fundamentals as I see them are quite solid.

The main risks to Vår Energi AS, given the mergers and the size and scale advantages the company now operates at, are, in my view, limited to the main correlations. As we've seen with the recent geopolitical trends, the company's share price has gone down in conjunction with the oil price and with the gas price uncertainty. The forecasts we have for the company, which I'll get into, almost can't be argued to be forecasts to heed at a 100% failure rate.

The uncertainty is, therefore, the main problem - and it's this that we must price for.

So, let's price it.

Vår Energi Valuation and upside - here, we can invest.

One of the trickiest questions I considered when looking at Vår Energi was whether to consider the company speculative, not because of its size, but because of end markets and correlations. In the end, I chose not to do so. The size and the breadth of its operations would make such a classification unfair.

But I want to emphasize that Vår Energi is quite volatile, as we've seen in the past 3 years. The current upside is very exuberant if you were to listen to the forecasts and where the company typically trades. Even taking the lowest historical P/E rating and discounting it further by 10-20% brings us to a P/E of 13.5x on a forward basis, and even there, we see a 180 %+ total RoR potential to 2027E.

The native yield of this company is more than 16% at this time. Whenever you encounter a yield this size, there are risks involved - significant ones. No different for this one. If the oil price were to continue to drop, this company's profits could be "carved into", and that dividend would quickly need to be cut, which likely would result in further share price decline. So, the forecast you see above, while not speculative, I would consider to be "optimistic" at the very least.

On the other hand, valuation.

Vår Energi is currently priced at only 0.76x sales. The more assets the company has and the longer the company has been trading like this, the lower those multiples will be. 1.69x EV/EBITDA, with a levered FCF yield of close to 30%.

Closest international and local competition? Vår Energi can be compared to Occidental (OXY), and Vår Energi is priced at half the multiples of OXY in terms of revenue and less than a quarter in terms of EBITDA. This company is cheap both in terms of forward multiples and in terms of peers; there's no doubt in my mind about that. (Source: Paywalled Tikr.com link)

It is, in fact, even cheaper priced than Aker BP at this time, another company I invest in. Street Target confirms this. 14 analysts follow Vår Energi, and they average at around 35-36 NOK/share, with 11 out of 14 analysts at a "Buy" or "Outperform" rating. The company isn't as cheap as it was in September, in terms of Price/NAV (currently 1.02x), but it's cheaper than it was only 1-2 months ago.

I believe the time has now come to "BUY" Vår Energi AS - and I am taking the plunge. As of this morning, I am adding 0.1% to my portfolio in Vår Energi AS for the long term, and I am giving the company a long-term PT of buying below 36 NOK for the native for an upside to an 8-9x 2026-2027 P/E with a possible FV as high as 60 NOK (But again, very optimistic).

The company's ADR is called VARRY, and it's a 2x ADR, which means that 1 VARRY equals 2 native shares of VAR. That means my 36 NOK price target comes to a VARRY target of $6.63/share. However, given the thin trading of the company's ADR, I would only consider the native.

My introductory thesis for Vår Energi is as follows.

Thesis

  • Vår Energi is a fundamentally attractive oil & gas company. With the current geopolitical uncertainties, I have been expecting declines in local companies in the EU, Asia, Africa, and other areas, and I have not been disappointed in what has been happening.
  • Vår Energi has now declined to below 29 NOK/share for the native ticker, and in any oil/gas or forecast case, this marks an attractive thesis and upside for me. I therefore rate this company as a "BUY" at this time.
  • The forecasts have now been, in my view, adjusted down to give an upside even in the case of very conservative scenarios. I forecast Vår Energi at a 13x forward P/E, and based on the company's forward earnings in 2026-2027, this comes to an implied share price of over 60 NOK for the native. I give the company a PT of 36 NOK or below, where it is an attractive buy, taking into consideration potential downsides in oil price and geopolitical trends.
  • This company has a triple-digit potential upside.

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (italicized).

  • This company is qualitative
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative & well-run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic upside based on earnings growth or multiple expansions/reversions.

The company fulfills 3 out of 3 quality indicators and 1 out of 2 valuation indicators. That means 4/5 "stars" here, which is enough for an attractive thesis for the company. Based on this, and a valuation target of 36 NOK/share where I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that's attractive enough for me. I give the company a "Buy" rating as of April 2025.
